Isolation of mitochondrial subpopulations from skeletal muscle: 
optimizing recovery and preserving integrity N Lai1,2,3, C Kummitha1,2,3, MG Rosca7, H Fujioka5, B Tandler8, and C Hoppel4,5,6
1Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Old Dominion University, Norfolk, Virginia 2Biomedical Engineering Institute, Old Dominion University, Norfolk, Virginia 3Department of Biomedical Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio

Medicine, Cleveland, Ohio Abstract
Aim.— The subsarcolemmal (SSM) and interfibrillar (IFM) mitochondria in skeletal muscle 
appear to have distinct biochemical properties affecting metabolism in health and disease The 
isolation of mitochondrial subpopulations has been a long-time challenge while the presence of a 
continuous mitochondrial reticulum challenges the view of distinctive SSM and IFM 
bioenergetics Here, a comprehensive approach is developed to identify the best conditions to 
separate mitochondrial fractions Methods.— The main modifications to the protocol to isolate SSM and IFM from rat skeletal 
muscle were: a) decreased dispase content and homogenization speed; b) trypsin treatment of SSM 
fractions; c) recentrifugation of mitochondrial fractions at low speed to remove subcellular 
components To identify the conditions preserving mitochondrial function, integrity, and 
maximizing their recovery, microscopy (light and electron) were used to monitor effectiveness and 
efficiency in separating mitochondrial subpopulations while respiratory and enzyme activities 
were employed to evaluate function, recovery and, integrity Results.— With the modifications described, the total mitochondrial yield increased with 
recovery of 80% of mitochondria contained in the original skeletal muscle sample The difference

1 Introduction
After the development of the protocol for isolation of subsarcolemmal (SSM) and 
interfibrillar (IFM) mitochondria from heart,1 a similar method was used for isolation of 
SSM and IFM in skeletal muscle, showing biochemical differences between the two 
populations similar to those observed in heart muscle.2–4 However, the isolation of the entire 
population or the subpopulations of mitochondria from skeletal muscle has been a challenge 
Some isolation issues are related to the unique morphology of skeletal muscle in comparison 
to other tissues In particular, there are fewer mitochondria in skeletal muscle than in heart, 
kidney, or liver,5 also disruption of myofibrils and their filaments is more difficult to achieve 
than that of typical subcellular components in other tissues Skeletal muscle protocols do not 
provide high mitochondrial recovery because actin and myosin are not easily separated from 
these organelles in this tissue Previous electron microscopy of the skeletal muscle SSM 
fraction separated from the myofibrillar pellet using the Polytron (Brady L and Hoppel C., 
unpublished work) revealed the presence of an assortment of membrane fragments in this 
fraction The isolation of the two subpopulations of skeletal muscle mitochondria is based upon two 
steps: a) homogenization to disrupt the sarcolemma to release and separate SSM from 
myofibrils; b) enzymatic treatment to digest myofibrils and release IFM from their 
incarceration within the contractile elements An obstacle in the first step, is the separation 
of SSM from myofibrils without disrupting the integrity of the former It was reported that a 
combination of homogenization and use of dispase, a proteolytic enzyme, in the isolation 
procedure in skeletal muscle of humans and rodents improved the harvesting of SSM.6,7 
However, electron microscopy was not used to determine mitochondrial purity as previously 
reported for heart muscle.1 Because nagarse alters some of the mitochondrial properties 
related to fatty acid oxidation and transport,8,9 trypsin has replaced nagarse for both 
skeletal6,7,10 and heart muscle11, in the second step A major issue in isolating the entire mitochondrial population12 or the two 
subpopulations2–4,13,14 of these organelles from skeletal muscle is the low recovery

mitochondria.protocol:
**Steps:**

1. The main modifications to the protocol to isolate SSM and IFM from rat skeletal muscle were: 
   a) decreased dispase content and homogenization speed
   b) trypsin treatment of SSM fractions
   c) recentrifugation of mitochondrial fractions at low speed to remove subcellular components.

2. Microscopy (light and electron) were used to monitor effectiveness and efficiency in separating mitochondrial subpopulations.

3. Respiratory and enzyme activities were employed to evaluate function, recovery, and integrity.

4. With the modifications described, the total mitochondrial yield increased with recovery of 80% of mitochondria contained in the original skeletal muscle sample.

5. The difference between SSM and IFM oxidative capacity (10%) with complex-I substrate was significant only with a saturated ADP concentration.

6. The inner and outer membrane damage for both subpopulations was less than 1% and 8%, respectively, while the respiratory control ratio was 16.

**Conclusion:**
Using a multidisciplinary approach, conditions were identified to maximize SSM and IFM recovery while preserving mitochondrial integrity, biochemistry, and morphology. High quality and recovery of mitochondrial subpopulations allow the study of the relationship between these organelles and disease.

summary:
**Iteration 2:**

Revised Summary: This study aims to optimize the recovery and integrity of mitochondrial subpopulations in skeletal muscle. By modifying the isolation protocol, the researchers improve the isolation of subsarcolemmal (SSM) and interfibrillar (IFM) mitochondria. The study evaluates mitochondrial function using microscopy and enzyme activity, revealing differences in oxidative capacity, ADP concentration, and inner and outer membrane damage between SSM and IFM. These findings provide insights into the distinct biochemical properties of SSM and IFM and their role in metabolism and disease.

article:
Chunk # 1.
Most 
procedure protocols lead to recovery of 20–30% with only one particular experiment 
yielding a 50% recovery.15 This raises the concern as to how a small number of 
mitochondria can represent the entire sample An important issue in the isolation of Lai et al Page 2
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptmitochondria is the damage to the mitochondrial inner and outer membranes Thus, 
evaluation of the inner and outer membrane integrity should be systematically determined, 
which has not been done in previous studies.2–4
In the present work, a comprehensive approach was designed that combines light and 
electron microscopy and respiration and enzyme activities to identify the conditions a) to 
decrease dispase content and homogenization speed; b) for the use of trypsin treatment of 
SSM fractions; c) to remove intruding subcellular components by recentrifugation of 
mitochondrial fractions at low speed; d) maximize recovery while at the same time 
preserving integrity of the two populations 2 Results
The body weight of the Wistar rats at 23 weeks was 600±73 g Skeletal muscle 
mitochondrial subpopulations were isolated from vastus lateralis  and from gastrocnemius 
muscles (2 g wet tissue) using the protocol presented in Figure 1 These muscle groups were 
used to facilitate the comparison between the results of this work with those obtained by 
other investigators who used rat quadriceps3 and gastrocnemius2 muscles and because the 
vastus lateralis is typically used in human bioenergetics studies 2.1 Subcellular fractionation techniques
Morphological examination of in situ  skeletal muscle cells revealed the typical two 
populations of mitochondria (Figure 2) The effect of the first enzymatic treatment on the 
skeletal muscle tissue is shown in the EM micrographs of the dispase pellet (Fig 3a and 3b) 
The dispase protease treatment breaks the anchorage elements that keep the myofibers 
adherent and exposes the sarcolemma without the interposition of fibrous connective tissue 
Several experiments were conducted to optimize the amount of dispase (0.09–5 mg g−1), 
homogenizer speed (100–2200 rpm), and number of strokes (1–5) (Fig 1S and 2S) As 
shown in the supplemental data (Fig, 1S-2S), using the same dispase treatment and low 
homogenizer speed (<200 rpm) led to an incomplete separation of the SSM from 
myofibrillar pellet while higher homogenizer speed (<900 rpm) and number of strokes (>2) 
led to a disruption of the interfibrillar region with a consequent mixing of the two 
populations The disruption of the muscle fiber appeared more sensitive to the homogenizer 
speed and number of strokes when the concentration of dispase used to treat the sample was 
high The conditions to separate and isolate SSM and IFM were selected to obtain a) the 
highest state 3 respiration rate and respiratory control ratio (RCR); b) a separation of SSM 
from the myofibrillar pellet; c) minimal myofibrillar pellet disruption during the first 
homogenization To perform this step, light microscopy (Fig 1S, 2S) of myofibrillar pellet 
was used to monitor each condition to evaluate the degree of myofibrillar pellet disruption 
and the presence of residual SSM, while SSM respiration rates were measured 
polarographically It appears that the lower the dispase concentration, the higher respiration 
rate with a saturated concentration of ADP and RCR (Fig 3S) So our study focused on 
experimental conditions requiring a low concentration of dispase (<0.36 mg g−1) The 
optimal range of the homogenizer speed appears to be 700–900 rpm for dispase 
concentrations lower than 0.18 mg g−1 with one stroke The absence of SSM within the Lai et al Page 3
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptmyofibrillar pellet is shown by EM (Fig.4a and 4b), while the IFM are still embedded 
between the myofibrils Thereafter, the myofibrillar pellet is treated with trypsin to facilitate 
the release of IFM After two cycles of homogenization and centrifugation, a pellet with the 
IFM fraction is obtained The IFM is re-suspended, homogenized and centrifuged (Fig 1) to 
separate the IFM fraction from other subcellular components The effect of this separation is 
shown in the EM micrographs of the pellet following trypsin digestion (Fig 5) This figure 
shows aggregates of actin filaments with the presence of a few trapped mitochondria Both SSM and IFM pellets are resuspended and centrifuged for 5 minutes at low speed to 
pellet nuclei and myofibrillar fragments This short centrifugation leads to more efficient 
mitochondria purification in the subsequent resuspension and centrifugation cycles with CP2 
and KME (Fig 1) EM (Fig 6a and 6b) shows the purity of the isolated SSM and IFM 2.2 Mitochondrial content
The yield of the two mitochondrial populations was similar in both vastus lateralis and 
gastrocnemius (Table 1) For both muscles, the SSM yield is 35% that of the IFM yield

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:**

1. "The effect of the first enzymatic treatment on the skeletal muscle tissue is shown in the EM micrographs of the dispase pellet. The dispase protease treatment breaks the anchorage elements that keep the myofibers adherent and exposes the sarcolemma without the interposition of fibrous connective tissue."
2. "Several experiments were conducted to optimize the amount of dispase, homogenizer speed, and number of strokes. Using the same dispase treatment and low homogenizer speed (<200 rpm) led to an incomplete separation of the SSM from the myofibrillar pellet while higher homogenizer speed (<900 rpm) and number of strokes (>2) led to a disruption of the interfibrillar region with a consequent mixing of the two populations."
3. "The conditions to separate and isolate SSM and IFM were selected to obtain the highest state 3 respiration rate and respiratory control ratio (RCR) and a separation of SSM from the myofibrillar pellet with minimal disruption."
4. "To perform this step, light microscopy of the myofibrillar pellet was used to monitor each condition to evaluate the degree of myofibrillar pellet disruption and the presence of residual SSM, while SSM respiration rates were measured polarographically."
5. "The optimal range of the homogenizer speed appears to be 700–900 rpm for dispase concentrations lower than 0.18 mg g−1 with one stroke."
6. "The myofibrillar pellet is then treated with trypsin to facilitate the release of IFM."
7. "After two cycles of homogenization and centrifugation, a pellet with the IFM fraction is obtained."
8. "The IFM is re-suspended, homogenized, and centrifuged to separate the IFM fraction from other subcellular components."
9. "The SSM and IFM pellets are resuspended and centrifuged for 5 minutes at low speed to pellet nuclei and myofibrillar fragments."
10. "This short centrifugation leads to more efficient mitochondria purification in the subsequent resuspension and centrifugation cycles with CP2 and KME."
11. "The purity of the isolated SSM and IFM is confirmed by electron microscopy."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation from skeletal muscle tissue using dispase treatment, homogenization, centrifugation, and trypsin digestion. The protocol includes optimization steps for dispase amount, homogenizer speed, and number of strokes. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the specific concentrations of dispase, the duration of trypsin treatment, and the details of subsequent resuspension and centrifugation cycles with CP2 and KME.

summary: Most procedure protocols yield a recovery of 20-30%, with one experiment achieving 50% recovery. However, the concern arises regarding how a small number of mitochondria can represent the entire sample. The integrity of the mitochondrial inner and outer membranes should be evaluated systematically, which has not been done in previous studies. In this work, a comprehensive approach combining microscopy, respiration, and enzyme activities was designed to identify optimal conditions for isolation while preserving integrity. The Wistar rats' body weight was 600±73g at 23 weeks, and skeletal muscle mitochondrial subpopulations were isolated from vastus lateralis and gastrocnemius muscles. Morphological examination revealed two populations of mitochondria. The enzymatic treatment with dispase broke the anchorage elements and exposed the sarcolemma. Various experiments were conducted to optimize the amount of dispase, homogenizer speed, and number of strokes. The conditions were selected to achieve high respiration rates, separate subpopulations, and minimize myofibrillar pellet disruption. Trypsin treatment facilitated the release of the IFM fraction. The yield of both mitochondrial populations was similar in both muscles, with the SSM yield being 35% of the IFM yield.

Chunk # 2.
To 
quantify mitochondrial content in skeletal muscle, citrate synthase (CS) and succinate 
dehydrogenase (SDH) were used as marker enzymes No differences in the activities of CS 
and SDH were observed between the two mitochondrial fractions (Table 1) in both muscle 
groups The recovery of the total population of mitochondria based on the inner membrane-
bound enzyme (SDH) was approximately 75–83% (Table 1) 2.3 Mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation
In the presence of glutamate, the results obtained for oxidative phosphorylation of the SSM 
and IFM isolated from vastus lateralis are similar to those from the gastrocnemius (Table 2) 
State 3 respiratory rate of SSM with an unsaturated concentration of ADP is similar to that 
observed for IFM It should be noted that the SSM pellet was treated with trypsin during the 
first centrifugation at 7000 g to eliminate membranous material The effect of trypsin on the 
State 3 respiration rate and RCR was investigated within a concentration range of 0–10 mg g
−1 State 3 respiration rate in the presence of trypsin treatment was 20% higher than that in 
absence of trypsin Thus, the removal of non-mitochondrial membranes leads to a purer 
preparation State 4 respiration rate in both SSM and IFM was low Maximal respiratory rate of SSM 
obtained with a saturated concentration of ADP is significantly lower than that measured for 
IFM The uncoupled rate (DNP) for both SSM and IFM was significantly increased (15–
20%) compared to that with a saturated concentration of ADP, while oxidative 
phosphorylation and uncoupling respiratory rates of IFM are approximately 10% higher than 
those observed for SSM 2.4 Mitochondrial integrity
The RCR values indicate that SSM and IFM are highly coupled in both muscle groups 
(Table 2) The ADP/O ratios for SSM and IFM are similar to those reported in the literature 16 To show the degree of integrity of both populations of mitochondria, the respiration rate 
in the presence of NADH is less than 1% of that obtained with DOC, indicating highly intact Lai et al Page 4
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptinner mitochondrial membranes (Table 3) The integrity of the outer membrane of 
mitochondria also is well preserved, as the respiration rate in the presence of cytochrome c is 
5–8% of that obtained with DOC These results clearly indicate that the isolation procedures 
employed in this study lead to the isolation of very well-coupled subpopulations of 
mitochondria with intact inner and outer membranes 2.5 Electron transport chain and cytochrome c
The activities of the electron transport chain complexes responsible for electron flux in SSM 
were not different from those in IFM for both vastus lateralis and gastrocnemius (Table 4) 
Complex I activity was lower than that of NCR This difference is most likely 
methodological as we observe the same phenomena in our previous studies.6, 11 The 
complex V activity in SSM was lower than that observed in IFM for both muscles Also, in 
both muscles, the cytochrome assay showed that the content of cytochrome aa3, b, and c1 in 
SSM was similar to that measured for IFM (Figure 7) In contrast, cytochrome c content of 
IFM was significantly higher than that observed for SSM This difference was observed in 
both muscles 3 Discussion
This study provides a comprehensive approach for optimizing experimental conditions to 
isolate large amounts of skeletal muscle mitochondria with emphasis on obtaining separated 
subsarcolemmal and interfibrillar organelles without compromising integrity and respiration 
rates Mitochondrial recovery and the degree of integrity obtained with this protocol are 
higher than those previously reported for skeletal muscle.2–4,6 This isolation protocol 
revealed that the oxidative capacity of SSM with a complex I substrate is 10% less than that 
observed for IFM A combination of specific characteristics of the medium,12 
homogenization,18 proteolytic enzyme,19–21 and differential centrifugation22,23 has been 
used to isolate mitochondria from this tissue focusing on one population using protease.24,25 
In this work, salt-based medium was used rather than sucrose/mannitol-based because has 
been shown to be more suitable for isolation of skeletal muscle mitochondria.26–28 The 
focus of the study was on the homogenization and protease conditions to isolate both 
subpopulations of mitochondria In dog6 muscle study, we used dispase to separate SSM and 
IFM fractions and trypsin to isolate IFM Major modifications were: a) reduced amount of 
dispase used to treat the sample; b) homogenization to separate SSM from myofibrillar 
pellet was optimized at 750rpm using microscopy; c) SSM fraction was treated with trypsin 
as the IFM fraction without collagenase type 2; d) intermediate centrifugation at low g to 
separate out the heavy subcellular components Microscopy was essential to provide tangible 
evidence of an efficient separation of the two fractions of mitochondria

summary:
Revised Summary:
1. Mitochondrial content in skeletal muscle was quantified using citrate synthase (CS) and succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) enzymes. No differences in CS and SDH activities were observed between the two mitochondrial fractions.
2. The recovery of the total population of mitochondria based on SDH activity was approximately 75-83%.
3. Similar results were obtained for oxidative phosphorylation in different muscle groups. State 3 respiration rate, state 4 respiration rate, and maximal respiratory rate were measured.
4. Trypsin treatment increased the state 3 respiration rate and purity of the mitochondrial preparation by removing non-mitochondrial membranes.
5. There were differences in the respiration rate and uncoupling rate between subsarcolemmal mitochondria (SSM) and interfibrillar mitochondria (IFM). The uncoupled rate (DNP) was significantly increased compared to the rate with a saturated concentration of ADP.
6. Both SSM and IFM showed high coupling and integrity of the inner and outer mitochondrial membranes. The RCR values indicated high coupling, and the respiration rates in the presence of NADH and cytochrome c confirmed the intactness of the membranes.
7. The activities of the electron transport chain complexes responsible for electron flux were similar in SSM and IFM. Complex I activity was lower than that of NCR, and complex V activity was lower in SSM. The content of cytochrome aa3, b, and c1 was similar in SSM and IFM, while cytochrome c content was higher in IFM.
8. A comprehensive approach using a salt-based medium and specific homogenization conditions was employed for isolating skeletal muscle mitochondria. Dispase, collagenase type 2, and differential centrifugation were used to separate SSM and IFM fractions, and microscopy confirmed the efficient separation of the two fractions.

Chunk # 3.
3.1 Mitochondrial yield and recovery
The experimental conditions were optimized to enhance the yield of both SSM and IFM 
Electron microscopy was essential to optimize the experimental conditions in the first step 
and to provide evidence of mitochondrial purity as previously reported for heart muscle;1 
this technique was not used for skeletal muscle in which actin and myosin are not easy to 
separate from mitochondria Centrifugation at low g for both SSM and IFM fractions before Lai et al Page 5
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptthe washing cycles at moderate centrifugation speed allows an efficient removal of heavier 
subcellular components from mitochondria Another factor affecting SSM yield is related to 
the use of dispase, which separates the adherent myofibrils before the first homogenization 
of the tissue The SSM yield determined in this work was almost 4.7-fold higher than that 
obtained in the absence of dispase treatment using Potter-Elvehjem4 and 1.4 fold higher than 
that obtained using the Polytron2,3 Also, the IFM yield was 2.3–3.1 fold higher than that 
obtained in previous studies.2–4 The overall mitochondrial recovery was the highest reported 
(75–83%) among protocols for isolation of SSM and IFM using a few grams of tissue For 
the isolation of all mitochondria in small samples, a specific protocol was developed to 
obtain a mitochondrial recovery of 40–50% of the homogenate of 25–100mg of skeletal 
muscle tissue,15,21 whereas protocols employing a few grams of tissue yield only 20–30% 12,29 For the isolation of SSM and IFM previous protocols obtained a mitochondrial 
recovery of less than 40%.2–4,14
In the present study, the difference between the mitochondrial recovery obtained with CS 
and SDH could be related to a leakage of mitochondrial CS A loss of 12–17% of malate 
dehydrogenase and citrate synthase from rat skeletal muscle mitochondria has been 
previously reported.30 In the same work, the loss of CS content, attributed to mitochondrial 
leakage, was compatible with the intactness of mitochondria membranes Also, the slight 
damage of the mitochondrial membrane observed in our study suggested that the CS 
leakage, if present, does not significantly affect mitochondrial integrity A significant 
leakage of soluble enzymes would be associated with swollen mitochondria, low ADP/O, 
and RCR ratio Instead, our isolated SSM and IFM have very high respiration rates and RCR 
with normal ADP/O ratio, i.e., 2.8–2.9, while EM images showed that mitochondria were 
not swollen 3.2 Purity and integrity of mitochondria;
Both inner and outer membranes are intact (Table 3), with a low degree of damage compared 
to that previously reported for both SSM and IFM.3,4,6 In particular, the inner membrane 
damage was approximately 6% for SSM6 and 2–6% for IFM3,6 while in this work it was less 
than 1% for both mitochondria populations The outer membrane damage observed in 
previous studies on rat skeletal muscle was 10–30%,4,6 while in this work it was 5–8% It 
should be noted that the protocol used to quantify the intactness of mitochondria membrane 
varies among all previous studies Nevertheless, the lower integrity observed in the previous 
rat studies could be related to the use of nagarse which has been shown to produce a loss of 
mitochondrial matrix constituent.31 Homogenization conditions, i.e., rpm and loose pestle, 
of the tissue samples and the gentle resuspension of the pellet after each centrifugation are 
important factors affecting the integrity of mitochondria The low state 4 respiration rate in both subpopulations of mitochondria indicates that the 
isolation procedure did not result in an uncoupling effect Additionally, the high RCR (>16) 
for both subpopulations provided further evidence of coupled mitochondria The RCR is 
used to evaluate the quality of mitochondria Although a cutoff number so far has not been 
identified, it is accepted that the higher the RCR the higher the quality of the mitochondria 
In this study, for SSM, RCR was the highest value, i.e., 16, compared to those reported in Lai et al Page 6
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptliterature (5–13);2–4 for IFM, it was similar or higher, i.e., 16–26, to those previously 
reported (7–18).2–4 In these studies, RCR values appear to be determined with higher ADP 
concentration (0.4–0.6mM) than by us (0.2 mM) to determine state 3 respiratory rate ADP/O ratio for NADH-linked substrate in both subpopulations of mitochondria (2.8–2.9) 
was similar to or higher than that reported in other studies.16 We standardize our ADP as 
noted in the method section and include AMP While, the methods of mitochondrial isolation 
and respiration measurement are affecting this ratio, using standardized ADP is critical The 
high value of ADP/O reported in our study is consistent with good integrity of the isolated

mitochondria.protocol:
**Extracted Steps:**
1. Experimental conditions were optimized to enhance the yield of both SSM and IFM.
2. Electron microscopy was used to optimize the experimental conditions and provide evidence of mitochondrial purity.
3. Centrifugation at low g was performed for both SSM and IFM fractions before washing cycles at moderate centrifugation speed to remove heavier subcellular components.
4. Dispase was used to separate adherent myofibrils before the first homogenization of the tissue.
5. The SSM yield was 4.7-fold higher with dispase treatment compared to without, using Potter-Elvehjem and 1.4-fold higher using the Polytron.
6. The IFM yield was 2.3-3.1 fold higher compared to previous studies.
7. The overall mitochondrial recovery was the highest reported (75-83%) for SSM and IFM isolation using a few grams of tissue.
8. A specific protocol was developed to obtain a mitochondrial recovery of 40-50% from small samples of 25-100mg of skeletal muscle tissue, while protocols using a few grams of tissue yielded only 20-30%.
9. The difference in mitochondrial recovery between CS and SDH assays could be due to a leakage of mitochondrial CS.
10. The slight damage to the mitochondrial membrane observed did not significantly affect mitochondrial integrity.
11. The isolated SSM and IFM showed high respiration rates, normal ADP/O ratio, and were not swollen.
12. Both inner and outer membranes of the mitochondria were intact, with less than 1% damage to the inner membrane and 5-8% damage to the outer membrane.
13. The homogenization conditions and gentle resuspension of the pellet after each centrifugation were important factors affecting mitochondrial integrity.
14. The low state 4 respiration rate indicated that the isolation procedure did not result in an uncoupling effect.
15. The high RCR (>16) for both SSM and IFM indicated coupled mitochondria.
16. The RCR values were higher compared to previous studies, and the ADP/O ratio was similar or higher.
17. Standardized ADP and inclusion of AMP were critical for determining the ADP/O ratio.
18. The high ADP/O ratio indicated good integrity of the isolated mitochondria.

**Incompleteness/Uncertainties:**
- The specific steps for optimizing experimental conditions and using electron microscopy are not mentioned.
- Details about the washing cycles and dispase treatment are not provided.
- The specific protocols for obtaining mitochondrial recovery from small samples and using a few grams of tissue are not described.
- The method used to quantify the intactness of mitochondria membrane is not specified.
- The cutoff number for RCR as an indicator of mitochondrial quality is not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides several steps for mitochondrial isolation, including optimization of experimental conditions, centrifugation, dispase treatment, and assessment of mitochondrial yield and recovery. However, there are gaps in the protocol, such as the lack of specific details for certain steps and missing information about protocol variations used in previous studies. The article emphasizes the high mitochondrial recovery and integrity achieved in the isolation procedure.

article:
Chunk # 4.
SSM characteristics
In our study, the respiration rate of the two populations was similar in the presence of an 
unsaturated (0.2mM) concentration of ADP, but a difference was observed with a saturated 
(2 mM) concentration of ADP (Table 2) Because the mitochondrial respiration rate depends 
on ADP availability,32 mitochondria were stimulated with unsaturated or saturated ADP 
With unsaturated ADP, the absence of a respiration rate difference between SSM and IFM 
was reported in a rat skeletal muscle study10 when mitochondrial were stimulated with a low 
ADP concentration [0.085–0.3mM]; for saturated ADP, differences between the two types of 
mitochondria were observed with an ADP concentration of [0.4–0.6] mM2–4,13 
Nevertheless, a systematic comparison with earlier work is not possible because these 
previous studies did not investigate the effect of ADP concentration Under saturated ADP concentration, the lower respiration rate in SSM than that observed in 
IFM (Tab 2) is unrelated to the phosphorylation process because the respiration rate 
difference between the two persists with uncoupling Also, biochemical properties of the 
main component of the electron transport chain (Tab 3) are not responsible for this 
difference because the activity of complex I, II, and IV , as well as flavin protein domain of 
complex I- (NFR) and I-III (NCR) of SSM, were similar to those measured for IFM, 
indicating similar electron transport chain component characteristics in SSM and IFM The 
respiration rate difference should not be attributed to effects of trypsin because both 
mitochondrial fractions were treated with the same trypsin concentration Similar 
approaches were successfully used to exclude the possibility that differences between the 
two populations of mitochondria were due to the isolation procedures.33,34 It should be 
noted that the SSM respiration rate was 90% of that of IFM, while in a previous work in 
which SSM was not treated with trypsin, SSM respiration rate was 80% of that of IFM 
Thus, in our work the trypsin treatment of the SSM fraction appears to enhance the purity of 
SSM To further investigate the difference in maximal oxidation, cytochrome content was 
determined in both mitochondrial populations The reduced content of cytochrome c and c1 
found in SSM in comparison to that observed for IFM is consistent with a lower respiration 
rate of SSM compared to that of IFM Experimental35,36 and computational37–39 studies 
support a strong relationship between cytochrome c content and respiratory capacity In a rat 
kidney study from our laboratory, a decrease in oxidative phosphorylation was reported to be 
related to a reduced cytochromes c content of mitochondria with intact outer membranes.35 Lai et al Page 7
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author ManuscriptAlso, in a rat skeletal muscle study, a twofold increase in the respiratory capacity in white, 
red, and mixed muscle fibers was attributed to an approximately twofold increase of citrate 
synthase, cytochrome c, and cytochromes c oxidase.36 Computational studies confirmed a 
close relationship among cytochrome c reduced and oxidized concentrations and 
mitochondrial respiration rate.37–39
In addition to its role in facilitating the mobilization of electrons through the electron 
transport chain, cytochrome c also is involved in apoptosis The presence of a different level 
of cytochrome c in the two populations also was reported in skeletal muscle regarding 
mitochondrial susceptibility to apoptotic stimuli.40,41 A distinctive role of SSM and IFM in 
promoting apoptotic signals in skeletal muscle has been reported in which IFM have a 
predominant proapoptotic initiation activity.40
It has been proposed that mitochondria form a continuous reticulum.42–44 In support of 
compartmentalized oxidative and phosphorylation processes, a study performed on mouse 
skeletal muscle reported complex IV and V distribution obtained by analysis of confocal 
images of muscle fibers,42 showed localized differences in the reticulum: distribution of 
complex IV and V in the mitochondria neighboring blood vessels differed from that of the 
IFM In the periphery of the cell, paravascular mitochondria are rich in complex IV whereas 
the more central portions, i.e., IFM, have a higher amount of complex V  In our work, the 
activity of complex V is higher in IFM than SSM and consistent with the observation that 
the complex is more concentrated in the central region of the reticulum

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Mitochondria were stimulated with unsaturated or saturated ADP concentrations.
2. With unsaturated ADP, there was no difference in respiration rate between SSM and IFM.
3. With saturated ADP, there were differences in respiration rate between SSM and IFM.
4. The respiration rate difference between SSM and IFM persisted even with uncoupling.
5. The activity of complex I, II, IV, NFR, and NCR in SSM was similar to that of IFM.
6. The respiration rate difference between SSM and IFM was not due to trypsin treatment.
7. Trypsin treatment of the SSM fraction enhanced the purity of SSM.
8. SSM had a lower content of cytochrome c and c1 compared to IFM.
9. A decrease in oxidative phosphorylation was related to reduced cytochrome c content.
10. Cytochrome c content is strongly related to respiratory capacity.
11. Cytochrome c is involved in apoptosis and its level differs between SSM and IFM.
12. IFM have a predominant proapoptotic initiation activity compared to SSM.
13. Complex IV and V distribution differs between SSM and IFM.
14. Complex V activity is higher in IFM compared to SSM.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides several steps related to mitochondrial isolation, including the stimulation of mitochondria with unsaturated or saturated ADP concentrations, the measurement of respiration rates, and the comparison of biochemical properties and cytochrome content between SSM and IFM. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, and there are some gaps in the described steps.

summary: Summary 7: A study compared the respiration rate of two types of mitochondria, SSM and IFM, in skeletal muscle. The respiration rate difference between SSM and IFM persisted even with uncoupling and was unrelated to the phosphorylation process. Trypsin treatment enhanced the purity of SSM and increased its respiration rate. SSM had a lower content of cytochrome c and c1 compared to IFM, correlating with its lower respiration rate. Cytochrome c was involved in apoptosis, and its level differed between SSM and IFM. Complex IV and V distribution in the reticulum of mitochondria differed between SSM and IFM, with IFM having a higher amount of complex V. The study also found that the concentration of ADP affected mitochondrial respiration. Additionally, the activity of complex I and the biochemical properties of the electron transport chain were investigated in skeletal muscle. Computational studies supported a relationship between cytochrome c concentration and respiratory capacity. Trypsin was used in the isolation procedures for mitochondria, and the study referenced previous research on ADP availability and skeletal muscle respiration rates.

article:
Chunk # 5.
Although the 
activity of complex IV is similar in the two populations of mitochondria (unlike that 
observed by Glancy et al.42), the proportions of complex IV and V are different in SSM and 
IFM and consistent with a different electron transport chain distribution of mitochondria.42
Substrate transport and metabolism differences in mitochondria between peripheral and 
central fiber regions can coexist even in the presence of a mitochondria reticulum to 
accommodate morphological changes to conditions such as exercise training44–46 and 
disease.41 While there is still a debate as to the existence of two subpopulations of 
mitochondria,47–49 it has been shown that the subpopulations have different morphological 
characteristics50,51 and responses to exercise training.44–46 It is not clear how the concept of 
two distinct populations of mitochondria fit into the scheme of a mitochondria reticulum 
Reconciliation of these two concepts of skeletal muscle mitochondria is important because 
these organelles play a key role in the homeostasis of muscle cells, with SSM phenotypes 
different from that of IFM in healthy and disease states.41 For instance, there is evidence that 
in type 2 diabetes the diseases differentially affects the two populations of mitochondria 7,9,52 The method presented in this study provides an optimal design for separating the 
subpopulations of mitochondria in skeletal muscle This approach may prove 
complementary to the study of the function and structure of mitochondria reticula 3.4 Isolated mitochondria vs permeabilized fibers
The degree of mitochondrial integrity obtained with current protocols has been questioned,31 
with less disruptive approaches involving permeabilized fibers being favored.53 Other 
studies reported bioenergetics differences between permeabilized fiber and isolated 
mitochondria suggesting that the disruption of mitochondrial structure during the isolation Lai et al Page 8
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptprocess might alter mitochondria function probably because of a loss of matrix constituents 31 Nevertheless, inner and outer mitochondrial membrane integrity was not evaluated in 
those studies In a heart study by our group, it was shown that whereas the isolated 
mitochondria become spheroid their cristae were 77% lamelliform, as were those in the 
SSM in in situ  fibers.54 In a heart muscle study, the respiratory capacity in isolated 
mitochondria was the same as that observed in fibers.55
3.5 Limitations
Large amounts of tissue, skill and time needed to perform the protocol are obstacles in 
isolating both populations of mitochondria in comparison to permeabilized fibers Although 
muscle fibers provide a simpler approach with a small amount of tissue, the respiration rate 
reflects both subpopulations of mitochondria However, this approach cannot determine 
whether the defect is in both populations or only in one Bioenergetics in isolated 
mitochondria does not take into consideration the influence of the cytoskeleton and 
endoplasmic reticulum as do muscle fibers The absence of these influences could be 
advantageous or disadvantageous according to the hypothesis tested In this protocol, experimental conditions were optimized for 2 grams of skeletal muscle 
mass Nevertheless, the protocol could be scaled down to isolate SSM and IFM from small 
muscle samples (400mg) Subpopulations of heart mitochondria have been harvested from 
400mg.56 It should be noted that the use of small amounts of tissue as is the case with needle 
biopsy allows obtaining a small amount of mitochondria, which limits the number of 
bioenergetics assays 4 Conclusion
A comprehensive approach that combines light and electron microscopy, centrifugation 
separation, polarography, and enzymatic assays is ideal to optimize the experimental 
conditions used to isolate high quantity and quality of subpopulations of mitochondria to 
study the relationship between function and structure of skeletal muscle organelles This 
may be particularly important in determining the role of specific subpopulations of 
mitochondria in muscle diseases 5 Methods
5.1 Materials
Reagents: Dispase (neutral protease) was obtained from Worthington Biochemical 
Corporation (Lakewood, NJ, USA) Unless otherwise indicated, all other reagents were 
purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA) Buffers: The relaxing buffers, Chappell–Perry (CP) (100 mM KCl, 50 mM MOPS, 5 mM 
MgSO4, 1mM ATP) and CP2 (Buffer CP plus 0.2% defatted BSA and 1 mM EGTA), as 
well as KME (100 mM KCl, 50 mM MOPS and 0.5 mM EGTA, pH 7.4) were prepared for 
tissue storage, mitochondrial isolation and storage.57,58 The respiration buffer (80 mM KCl, 
50 mM MOPS, 1 mM EGTA, 5 mM KH2PO4, and 1 mg mL−1 defatted BSA, pH 7.4) was 
used for mitochondrial oxygen uptake measurements.59Lai et al Page 9
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript5.2 Animal model
The experimental protocols conformed to the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory 
Animals published by the National Research Council60 and were approved by the Case 
Western Reserve University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee Wistar colony 
rats were obtained from Charles River Laboratories, Wilmington, MA

**Steps:**

2. "Reagents: Dispase (neutral protease) was obtained from Worthington Biochemical Corporation (Lakewood, NJ, USA) Unless otherwise indicated, all other reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA)"
3. "Buffers: The relaxing buffers, Chappell–Perry (CP) (100 mM KCl, 50 mM MOPS, 5 mM MgSO4, 1mM ATP) and CP2 (Buffer CP plus 0.2% defatted BSA and 1 mM EGTA), as well as KME (100 mM KCl, 50 mM MOPS and 0.5 mM EGTA, pH 7.4) were prepared for tissue storage, mitochondrial isolation and storage."
4. "The respiration buffer (80 mM KCl, 50 mM MOPS, 1 mM EGTA, 5 mM KH2PO4, and 1 mg mL−1 defatted BSA, pH 7.4) was used for mitochondrial oxygen uptake measurements."
5. "The experimental protocols conformed to the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals published by the National Research Council and were approved by the Case Western Reserve University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee."
6. "Wistar colony rats were obtained from Charles River Laboratories, Wilmington, MA."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some information about the materials and buffers used for mitochondrial isolation, but it does not provide a detailed protocol for the isolation steps. Therefore, the protocol gaps are significant, and additional information is needed to perform the mitochondrial isolation.

summary: Revised Summary: This study conducted by Glancy et al. focused on subsarcolemmal (SSM) and interfibrillar (IFM) populations of mitochondria in skeletal muscle. The researchers found that the activity of complex IV is similar in both populations, but the proportions of complex IV and V differ. They also observed that diseases affect the two populations differently. The study emphasized the importance of matrix constituents and inner and outer mitochondrial membrane integrity. The researchers used various methods, including light microscopy, electron microscopy, centrifugation, polarography, and enzymatic assays, to study the function and structure of mitochondria. The study was conducted using Wistar colony rats as an animal model. The researchers discussed the role of specific subpopulations of mitochondria in muscle diseases and proposed methods for isolating and measuring their function and structure in skeletal muscle.

article:
Chunk # 6.
Three pairs of male 
Wistar (W) rats were housed in the Animal Resource Center facilities of Case Western 
Reserve University with 12:12-h light-dark cycle and were fed a standard diet chow (Prolab 
Isopro RMH 3000, LabDiet, St Louis, MO) The rats were euthanized at 23 wk (n=6) of 
age 5.3 Isolation of mitochondria
Skeletal muscle SSM and IFM were isolated from the vastus lateralis and the gastrocnemius 
using a previously described protocol1,6 with several important modifications to improve the 
mitochondrial isolation procedure (See Figure 1) After fat and connective tissue were 
removed, 2 g wet skeletal muscle was blotted dry and weighed for each preparation To 
separate fibers, the tissue was minced and suspended in cold (4 °C) buffer (CP) (20 ml), then 
incubated with 0.09 mg (1 U) dispase per gram wet weight tissue (g tissue) in CP buffer with 
stirring on ice for 10 min Out of the multiple proteases tested to break the anchorage 
elements that keep the myofibers adherent, dispase, a neutral dispase, provided the best 
separation of the fibers, exposing the sarcolemma (Fig.3) rendering it susceptible to 
disruption After removing dispase, gentle homogenization releases subsarcolemmal 
mitochondria, preserving the skinned myofibrills Subsequently, 20 ml of CP2 buffer g tissue
−1 was added to the sample and centrifuged at 7650 g for 10 min The pellet was 
resuspended in CP2 buffer (20 mL g tissue−1) and homogenized with a loose-fitting pestle 
(Potter-Elvehjem) at 750 rpm (Fisher Maxima Overhead Stirrer) with one stroke and 
immediately centrifuged for 10 min at 580 g The supernatant was separated from the 
myofibrillar pellet and treated with trypsin (6 mg/120000 BAEE units g tissue−1) during 
centrifugation at 7000 g for 20 min to collect the SSM fraction The SSM pellet was 
resuspended in CP2 buffer (10 ml g tissue−1) and centrifuged at 630 g for 5 minutes and the 
supernatant containing the SSM fraction collected The myofibrillar pellet was resuspended 
in CP buffer (10 mL g tissue−1), treated with trypsin (6 mg/120000 BAEE units g tissue−1) 
for 10 minutes, and homogenized at 1600 rpm with three strokes of a tight-fitting pestle 
(Potter-Elvehjem) An equal volume of CP2 buffer was added to the homogenate, which was 
centrifuged for 10 min at 11,950 g The pellet was resuspended in CP2 buffer (10 ml g tissue
−1), homogenized at 1600 rpm with three strokes of a tight-fitting pestle and centrifuged at 
345 g for 10 min The supernatant was collected by filtering through two layers of gauze and 
kept on ice The pellet was again resuspended in CP2 buffer (10 ml g tissue−1) and 
homogenized at 1600 rpm with three strokes of a tight-fitting pestle (Potter-Elvehjem), and 
centrifuged at 345 g for 10 minutes The supernatant collected by filtering through two 
layers of gauze was added to the first supernatant with the IFM fraction, then the combined 
fractions centrifuged at 7000 g for 20 min The IFM pellet was resuspended in CP2 buffer 
(10 ml g tissue−1) and centrifuged at 345 g for 5 minutes (trypsin pellet of IFM), and the 
supernatant containing IFM fraction was collected.Lai et al Page 10
Acta Physiol (Oxf)  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2020 February 01 Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author ManuscriptBoth SSM and IFM fractions were resuspended first with 10 ml g tissue−1 of CP2 buffer and 
then with 5 ml g tissue−1 of buffer BSA free (KME) at 7,000 g for 20 and 10 minutes, 
respectively The KME buffer was used to resuspend the final pellets to a concentration of 
approximately 30–40 mg mitochondrial protein ml−1 Ice-cold conditions were maintained 
throughout the SSM and IFM isolation procedure The Lowry method was used to determine 
mitochondrial protein concentration.61
5.4 Oxidative phosphorylation
Oxygen consumption by SSM and IFM was measured with a Clark-type electrode (YSI 
model 53) in a final volume of 0.5 mL respiration buffer57,62 at 30ºC in the presence of the 
substrate, glutamate (20 mM) Glutamate was the oxidative substrate used because it has its 
own transporter through the inner membrane and in the matrix, it is dehydrogenated by 
glutamate dehydrogenase yielding NADH, which is then oxidized by complex I of the ETC 
Among the complex I substrates it is the only substrate that does not require any further 
additions The concentration of glutamate, uncoupler, and ADP refers to the initial 
concentrations in the chamber An ADP concentration of 0.1 mM was used to deplete 
endogenous substrates before glutamate was added and the mitochondria stimulated with 0.2 
mM ADP (ADP-stimulated) to measure the State 3 respiration rate.63 After ADP depletion, 
State 4 respiration was measured (ADP-limited) The mitochondria State 3 and 4 respiration 
rates were measured twice To determine maximal oxidative phosphorylation, the respiratory 
rate also was measured in the presence of a saturated concentration of ADP, 2 mM

mitochondria.protocol:
Isolation of Mitochondria Protocol:

1. Remove fat and connective tissue from 2 g wet skeletal muscle.
2. Mince the tissue and suspend it in cold buffer (CP) (20 ml).
3. Incubate the tissue with 0.09 mg (1 U) dispase per gram wet weight tissue in CP buffer with stirring on ice for 10 min.
4. Remove dispase and perform gentle homogenization to release subsarcolemmal mitochondria.
5. Add 20 ml of CP2 buffer per gram tissue to the sample and centrifuge at 7650 g for 10 min.
6. Resuspend the pellet in CP2 buffer (20 mL per gram tissue) and homogenize with a loose-fitting pestle at 750 rpm.
7. Centrifuge the homogenate at 580 g for 10 min to separate the supernatant from the myofibrillar pellet.
8. Treat the supernatant with trypsin (6 mg/120000 BAEE units per gram tissue) during centrifugation at 7000 g for 20 min to collect the subsarcolemmal mitochondria (SSM) fraction.
9. Resuspend the SSM pellet in CP2 buffer (10 ml per gram tissue) and centrifuge at 630 g for 5 minutes to collect the SSM fraction.
10. Resuspend the myofibrillar pellet in CP buffer (10 ml per gram tissue), treat with trypsin (6 mg/120000 BAEE units per gram tissue) for 10 minutes, and homogenize at 1600 rpm with a tight-fitting pestle.
11. Add an equal volume of CP2 buffer to the homogenate and centrifuge at 11,950 g for 10 min.
12. Resuspend the pellet in CP2 buffer (10 ml per gram tissue), homogenize with a tight-fitting pestle, and centrifuge at 345 g for 10 min.
13. Collect the supernatant by filtering through two layers of gauze and combine it with the SSM fraction.
14. Centrifuge the combined fractions at 7000 g for 20 min to collect the interfibrillar mitochondria (IFM) fraction.
15. Resuspend the IFM pellet in CP2 buffer (10 ml per gram tissue) and centrifuge at 345 g for 5 minutes to collect the IFM fraction.
16. Resuspend both the SSM and IFM fractions first with 10 ml per gram tissue of CP2 buffer, and then with 5 ml per gram tissue of buffer BSA free (KME) at 7000 g for 20 and 10 minutes, respectively.
17. Maintain ice-cold conditions throughout the isolation procedure.
18. Determine mitochondrial protein concentration using the Lowry method.

Conclusion:
The protocol for isolating mitochondria from skeletal muscle tissue is provided in the article. The steps are described in detail, including the use of dispase for separating fibers, homogenization, centrifugation, and trypsin treatment. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the composition and preparation of the CP and CP2 buffers, as well as the specific modifications made to the previously described protocol. These missing details may limit the reproducibility of the protocol.

summary: Summary 6: Male Wistar rats, housed in the Animal Resource Center facilities of Case Western Reserve University, were fed a standard diet chow and euthanized to isolate mitochondria from skeletal muscle fibers. The isolation procedure involved the use of proteases and separation of fibers. Subsarcolemmal mitochondria (SSM) and interfibrillar mitochondria (IFM) fractions were obtained using the CP buffer and trypsin treatment. The study focused on oxidative phosphorylation, measuring State 3 and State 4 respiration rates, as well as maximal oxidative phosphorylation using glutamate as a substrate. The protein concentration was determined using the Lowry method. Published by Lai et al. in Acta Physiol (Oxf).

Mitochondrial enzyme activities were measured as previously described.59,62,65,66 Briefly, 
fresh SSM and IFM (1 mg of mitochondrial protein) samples were treated with cholate (10 
mg cholate mg−1,67) and 25 mM KPi 2mM EDTA−1 buffer to a final volume of 1 mL and 
supplemented with mammalian protease inhibitor cocktail (10 μL mL−1) The samples were 
diluted 1:10 for the enzymatic assays The electron transport chain activity components were 
determined using biochemical kinetics principles in which donors and acceptors span 
specific regions of the electron transport chain.62,65,66 Assays are based on the oxidation-
reduction reactions of Coenzyme Q, oxidation of NADH, formation of 2-nitro-5-benzoic 
acid, reduction of cytochrome c and dichlorophenol-indophenol which are measured 
spectrophotometrically at 340, 412, 550, and 600 nm, respectively Rotenone-sensitive 
NADH-Q reductase (CI): it measures NADH oxidation coupled to the reduction of 
coenzyme Q with exogenous decylubiquinone and anitmycin A to inhibit complex III and in Lai et al Page 11

cytochrome c reductase (NCR) is designed to evaluate the linked activity of complexes I and 
III with a measure of reduction of cytochrome c, the acceptor for complex III In this case, 
the oxidation of NADH by complex I is coupled to the reduction of cytochrome c The 
reduction of cytochrome c is quantified in the presence of exogenous oxidized cytochrome c,

ferricyanide reductase (NFR); it evaluates the activity of the NADH-dehydrogenase, the first 
three subunits of complex I, including flavin-mononucleotide The assay measures NADH

Thenoyltrifluoroacetone-sensitive succinate-Q reductase + quinone (CII+Q 1) assay is

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Add 0.2 mM of the uncoupler dinitrophenol (DNP) to the chamber.
2. Measure the oxidative capacity of the electron transport chain using the Respiratory Control Ratio (RCR), which is the State 3 divided by State 4.
3. Determine the concentration of ADP using an enzymatic method and calculate the ADP/O ratio (ADP mole added per mole of oxygen atom consumed).
4. Report the respiratory rate as nAO min^-1 mg^-1, which is nano atoms of oxygen consumed per minute and mg of mitochondrial protein. Convert the rate to pmolO2 s^-1 mg^-1 using a conversion factor of 8.33.
5. Prepare samples by treating fresh SSM and IFM (1 mg of mitochondrial protein) samples with cholate (10 mg cholate mg^-1) and 25 mM KPi 2mM EDTA^-1 buffer, supplemented with mammalian protease inhibitor cocktail (10 μL mL^-1).
6. Dilute the samples 1:10 for the enzymatic assays.
7. Determine the activity components of the electron transport chain using biochemical kinetics principles. Assays are based on the oxidation-reduction reactions of Coenzyme Q, oxidation of NADH, formation of 2-nitro-5-benzoic acid, reduction of cytochrome c, and dichlorophenol-indophenol, measured spectrophotometrically at 340, 412, 550, and 600 nm, respectively.
8. Measure rotenone-sensitive NADH-Q reductase (CI) activity by measuring NADH oxidation coupled to the reduction of coenzyme Q with exogenous decylubiquinone and anitmycin A to inhibit complex III, in the absence and presence of rotenone (R) to inhibit complex I.
9. Measure rotenone-sensitive NADH-cytochrome c reductase (NCR) activity to evaluate the linked activity of complexes I and III, by measuring the reduction of cytochrome c, the acceptor for complex III, in the presence of exogenous oxidized cytochrome c, potassium cyanide to inhibit complex IV, and in the absence and presence of R.
10. Measure NADH-ferricyanide reductase (NFR) activity to evaluate the activity of the NADH-dehydrogenase, the first three subunits of complex I, by measuring NADH oxidation coupled to the reduction of ferricyanide (K 6Fe(CN) 6) in the presence of sodium azide (NaN 3) to inhibit complex IV.
11. Measure thenoyltrifluoroacetone-sensitive succinate-Q reductase (CII) activity by measuring succinate oxidation coupled to the reduction of coenzyme Q and dichlorophenol-indophenol, with and without thenoyltrifluoroacetone to inhibit complex II.
12. Measure thenoyltrifluoroacetone-sensitive succinate-Q reductase + quinone (CII+Q1) activity to measure total CII activity by supplementing with coenzyme Q1, with and without thenoyltrifluoroacetone.
13. Measure anitimycin A-sensitive decylubiquinol-cytochrome c reductase (CIII) activity by measuring the reduction of cytochrome c coupled to the oxidation of decylubiquinol to decylubiquinone in the presence of oxidized cytochrome c and potassium cyanide, with and without anitmycin A.
14. Determine cytochrome c oxidase (CIV) activity as the first order rate constant, with and without potassium cyanide.

Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscript Author Manuscriptrespiration buffer at 30ºC.59,62 Following addition of mitochondria, ADP (2 mM), NADH 
(2.8 mM), and cytochrome c (0.032 mM) were added sequentially to the chamber, then 
deoxycholate (DOC 120 mM of stock solution) was titrated with 1 µL additions until 
maximal respiration was achieved Typically, 5–6 µL were required to reach plateau In 
intact mitochondria, the inner membrane is impermeable to NADH while the outer 
membrane is impermeable to cytochrome c Thus, an increase of mitochondrial respiration 
after the addition of NADH is related to NADH oxidation due to an alteration of the inner 
mitochondrial membrane;70 the increase of mitochondrial respiration after the addition of 
cytochrome c is related to its reduction by NADH-cytochrome c reductase and subsequent 
oxidation by cytochrome oxidase and reflects alteration of the outer mitochondrial 
membrane The addition of deoxycholate permeabilizes inner and outer membranes, while 
the mitochondrial respiration reflects maximal oxidation of the NADH through complex IV  5.7 Light and Electron Microscopy
Isolated mitochondria were fixed in an equal volume of phosphate-buffered quarter-strength 
Karnovsky’s fixative using a method reported previously.71 Muscle tissue was fixed by 
immersion in triple aldehyde/DMSO,72 homogenates, pellets, and isolated mitochondria 
were fixed by immersion in phosphate-buffered quarter-strength Karnovsky’s fixative73 and 
processed according to a method previously reported.71 Sections 0.7 μm thick were stained 
with toluidine blue and examined by light microscopy Thin sections were sequentially 
stained with acidified uranyl acetate followed by a modification of Sato’s triple lead stain74 
and examined in an FEI Tecnai Spirit (T12) electron microscope with a Gatan US4000 
4kx4k CCD 5.8 Statistical Analysis
Mean and standard deviation were used for statistical analysis of the results This approach 
was justified by the normal distribution of the data verified using the Shapiro-Wilk test The 
student’s paired t test with two tails was used to evaluate the null hypothesis of no difference 
between SSM and IFM variables A paired test was used because each set of SSM and IFM 
data was obtained from the same tissue A difference of p < 0.05 was considered significant Supplementary Material

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Following addition of mitochondria, ADP (2 mM), NADH (2.8 mM), and cytochrome c (0.032 mM) were added sequentially to the chamber.
2. Deoxycholate (DOC 120 mM of stock solution) was titrated with 1 µL additions until maximal respiration was achieved. Typically, 5–6 µL were required to reach plateau.
3. The inner membrane of intact mitochondria is impermeable to NADH, while the outer membrane is impermeable to cytochrome c. An increase in mitochondrial respiration after the addition of NADH indicates NADH oxidation due to an alteration of the inner mitochondrial membrane. An increase in mitochondrial respiration after the addition of cytochrome c indicates its reduction by NADH-cytochrome c reductase and subsequent oxidation by cytochrome oxidase, reflecting an alteration of the outer mitochondrial membrane.
4. The addition of deoxycholate permeabilizes both the inner and outer membranes, allowing for maximal oxidation of NADH through complex IV.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide details on the composition or preparation of the respiration buffer or the method of isolation of mitochondria.

**Conclusion:** The steps provided for mitochondrial isolation are complete and clear. However, there are gaps in the protocol, as the article does not mention the composition or preparation of the respiration buffer or the method of isolation of mitochondria.

summary: Summary 5: Mitochondrial respiration was measured by sequentially adding ADP (2 mM), NADH (2.8 mM), and cytochrome c (0.032 mM) to the chamber. Deoxycholate (DOC 120 mM of stock solution) was titrated to achieve maximal respiration. The increase in mitochondrial respiration after the addition of NADH and cytochrome c indicated alterations in the inner and outer mitochondrial membranes. Deoxycholate permeabilized the membranes, allowing for maximal oxidation of NADH. Muscle tissue was used in the study. Fixed mitochondria were examined using light and electron microscopy with specific fixation and staining methods. The electron microscope used was the FEI Tecnai Spirit (T12) equipped with a Gatan US4000 4kx4k CCD camera. Statistical analysis, including the Shapiro-Wilk test and student's paired t-test, was performed to evaluate the data, confirming the normal distribution, and considering a p-value of less than 0.05 as significant.

summary:
Summary: 
Subsarcolemmal and intermyofibrillar mitochondria play distinct roles in regulating skeletal muscle fatty acid metabolism. Cardiac mitochondria in heart failure show a decrease in respirasomes and oxidative phosphorylation. Endurance training elicits differential responses in subsarcolemmal and intermyofibrillar mitochondria. Skeletal muscle subsarcolemmal mitochondrial compartment is altered during catch-up fat after caloric restriction. There are different methods available for the isolation and study of skeletal muscle mitochondria. Mitochondrial structure and function can be disrupted by standard isolation methods. Subsarcolemmal mitochondria exhibit greater protein specific activities and functional coupling when isolated with the proteolytic enzyme nagarse. There are biochemical differences between subsarcolemmal and interfibrillar mitochondria from rat cardiac muscle. Age-related decline of oxidative phosphorylation in rat kidney mitochondria is mediated by decreased cytochrome c. Different muscle types have varying respiratory capacities in response to exercise. Mitochondrial function and metabolism in various tissues, isolation and preparation methods for mitochondria, role of cytochromes in oxidative phosphorylation, effects of exercise and training on mitochondrial function, implications of altered mitochondrial function in heart failure, optimization of mitochondrial assays and measurements, subcellular compartmentalization of mitochondria, biochemical studies of mitochondrial respiratory control, mitochondrial structure and disruption during isolation, role of inorganic phosphate in metabolic control, proteolytic enzymes and their effects on mitochondria, age-related decline in mitochondrial function, respiratory capacity of different types of muscle, methods for assessing mitochondrial function in vitro, and impact of caloric restriction on mitochondrial metabolism are important areas of research. Mitochondrial metabolism in skeletal muscle, role of oxidative phosphorylation in heart failure, methods for isolating functional mitochondria, impact of caloric restriction on fatty acid metabolism, and quantitative estimation of cytochromes in mitochondria are additional important aspects to consider.
